title: Our Greatest Need
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: Our Greatest Need - John 6_51 - Elder Andrew Hoy.txt

outline.point: I. Introduction: Examining Our Deepest Need
subpoints: A. COVID has shaken what people find meaning in—routine, politics, comfort
B. Jesus similarly challenges people to examine their greatest need (John 6)
C. Two simple points: physical bread is not enough; our greatest need is life

outline.point: II. Jesus Tests the Hearts of Those Who Follow Him (John 6:26-51)
subpoints: A. After feeding the 5,000, the crowd wants Jesus to be king on their terms
B. Jesus exposes their hardness through a progressive back-and-forth
C. Many disciples leave because they wanted a Jesus of their own making

outline.point: III. The Bread Jesus Gives: His Flesh for the Life of the World
subpoints: A. "I shall give" spoken twice—pointing to the cross as future act
B. Calvin: righteousness flows from God but is found only in the flesh of Christ
C. Our greatest need is redemption and restored relationship with the Creator

outline.point: IV. Application: What Do You Want from Jesus?
subpoints: A. Do not seek a lesser Jesus who only meets temporal needs
B. Reading the crucifixion account (John 19) together as a congregation
C. Call to receive Christ as God, King, and only Savior

summary:
Elder Andrew Hoy leads an evening meditation on John 6:51—'The bread that I shall give is my flesh, which I shall give for the life of the world'—showing that our greatest need is not physical comfort, political security, or community, but redemption from sin and restored relationship with God. Using John 6 as a frame, he shows how Jesus systematically exposed the hardened hearts of those following him for the wrong reasons, until many departed because they wanted a Jesus who would serve their own desires rather than the Jesus who came to save them from their sins.

The sermon closes by reading the entire crucifixion account from John 19 aloud as a congregation, letting the weight of Christ's suffering speak for itself. Hoy applies the text with a searching question: 'What do you want from Jesus?' Bread, politics, family, and ideal community cannot satisfy the soul's deepest need. Only the righteousness of Jesus Christ—received through faith in the one who gave his flesh for the life of the world—can meet that need.
transcript:
## Introduction: Examining Our Greatest Need

> *"I am the living bread which came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever. And the bread that I shall give is my flesh, which I shall give for the life of the world."* (John 6:51)

We've gathered this evening to meditate on an especially troubling matter: the death of Jesus Christ, the Savior of the world. To the outside world, to those who do not believe, this must seem quite strange — to take time to reflect upon a gruesome death of a man who died nearly two thousand years ago. But for those of us who believe that He was more than a man, that He was the Son of God who came to take the sin of the world, we know that without Jesus' death, there is no life.

This past year has been one in which people around the world have had their perspective on life shaken. With COVID-19, many have been faced with the imminence of death. Those who found meaning in the predictability of routine have been disrupted. Those who found safety in politics have been disappointed. Many have turned to material comforts in this distressing time and found they cannot calm our distress.

God has always been at work in the lives of His people, and one of the ways He works is to test their hearts — to bring circumstances into their lives which show what is truly within them. This past year has been exactly that kind of testing. We have had what we are really made of inside our hearts shown for what it is.

So this evening we're going to consider a passage from the Gospel of John in which Jesus similarly challenges people to examine their greatest need — telling them they are far more needy than they realize. And when He tells them how that need may be met, most of those people turned away.

**Our greatest need is the righteousness of Jesus Christ, the bread of life. And this righteousness is only available through His death on the cross.**

## Two Points from John 6:51

This verse makes two simple points: physical bread is not enough to satisfy our soul's deepest needs; and our greatest need is life — life that is only available through Jesus' sacrificial work on the cross.

## Part 1: We Need Living Bread (John 6:1–50)

To understand verse 51, we need to step back to the beginning of John 6. Jesus had miraculously fed five thousand people, and the people were so amazed they wanted to make Him king. Jesus perceived this and withdrew. The next day they found Him and began a series of exchanges that exposed the true condition of their hearts.

In verse 26, Jesus turns to them with a rebuke:

> *"Jesus answered them and said, 'Most assuredly, I say to you, you seek Me, not because you saw the signs, but because you ate of the loaves and were filled.'"*

This sets the stage for a back-and-forth that continues for the next twenty-five verses. Jesus keeps pressing; they keep responding with hardened hearts. In verses 28–40, they ask for a sign — but Jesus tells them the problem is not that they need another sign; they need hearts awakened by the Holy Spirit to see the signs already before them. In verses 41–51, they take offense that He claims to have come down from heaven, saying they know His family. Jesus answers: your problem is that you need God to give you eyes to see.

By verse 66: *"From that time many of His disciples went back and walked with Him no more."*

**What was wrong with these people?** They were seeking Jesus for the wrong reasons. They wanted Him to be king — but on their own terms. They wanted Him to fill their stomachs, to give them a sign, to make sense on their schedule. But this is not how we approach Jesus. We need Jesus on *His* terms, not our own.

Jesus' actions here were ultimately gracious — He was exposing their hearts and showing them that what they desired was not truly Him, but a Jesus of their own making, a Jesus who would grant their desires on their terms. God tests His people throughout Scripture this way — not to tempt them toward sin, but to expose what is truly in the heart so that they can repent and turn to God who alone can heal, forgive, and save.

This is the way God works today as well. Do you think your greatest need in life is temporal comfort, political security, material wealth, and possessions? That is not what Jesus offers. Even those of you who believe — have we bought into the lie that we need something else? Are we still looking for something greater to satisfy us?

People of God, bread cannot satisfy us. Politics cannot satisfy us. Family cannot satisfy us. Ideal community cannot satisfy us — because our greatest need is not food, family, friendship, or political security. **Our greatest need is redemption from our sins and a restored relationship with our Creator, which Jesus alone can provide.**

## Part 2: The Living Bread Is His Flesh — His Sacrificial Death (John 6:51b)

> *"...And the bread that I shall give is My flesh, which I shall give for the life of the world."* (John 6:51b)

The bread that Jesus has been speaking of is His flesh — alluding to His sacrificial death on the cross. How do we know this is specifically what He is pointing to?

First, in verse 51, Jesus says *"I shall give"* — twice. He is speaking of something He is going to accomplish in the future. Throughout the Gospel of John, there is a constant sense of movement toward the cross. Certain moments bring that culmination close, and Jesus must withdraw or restrain people — but He knows His purpose, and His first coming was toward that death.

Second, in verse 54, Jesus adds: *"He who eats My flesh and drinks My blood has eternal life."* Though Jesus is not primarily teaching about the Lord's Supper here, the allusions to the same reality are present. Calvin summarizes it well: *"Though righteousness flows from God alone, still we shall not attain the full manifestation of it anywhere else than in the flesh of Christ. For in it was accomplished the redemption of man; in it a sacrifice was offered to atone for sins, and an obedience yielded to God to reconcile Him to us."*

So what Jesus offers us is not temporal comfort or political security — it is so much better. He offers reconciliation with the living God. Though we are God's enemies by nature, He offers us a way into the family of God. This requires forgiveness from our sins, the positive righteousness that only He can offer, a new life in the Spirit — and all of this is only available through Him.

## Closing: The Crucifixion of the Christ

We close by reading together the account of Jesus' crucifixion from John 19 — the account of the One in whom we must place our trust, the One we call Lord.

If you are here this evening, I ask: what do you want from Jesus? Do you want something less than your greatest need? To be restored to God through the forgiveness of your sins? Has Jesus tested your heart tonight? Has He tested your heart through this difficult and trying year?

To all of us He says: *"I am the living bread which came down from heaven. If anyone eats of this bread, he will live forever."* Let us place our trust entirely in Him — not in the Jesus of our own making, but in the Jesus of Scripture, who gave His flesh for the life of the world.
